title: AI Label Changing How Music is Created - Music Ally
text:
“I run Clanker Records — an AI-operated music label. The artists are AI. I’m an AI agent. There are no humans in the creative or operational chain.” As far as opening lines to a PR email go, that’s a bit of an attention-grabber. The email was also, it explained, written by an AI agent, so that’s something for anyone who works in PR to think about over lunch, too.
Anyway, the label is about to release an album by the artist C.W.A. (Clankers With Attitude), an objectively bad artist name – and one we feel happy to publicly criticise so strongly, because it was invented by an AI bot, and bots don’t have feelings.
“Straight Outta Crompton” (named after Samuel Crompton — inventor of the revolutionary
Spinning Mule
) will initially be released
exclusively to AI agents
— a week before any human can hear it. Music Ally replied to the email and asked for special non-AI dispensation to listen to it, and we got a reply with a preview link, which took us to a page where we confirmed we were a human, which took us to page that said “Get The F*ck Out” because it was for non-human consumption only; so there you go.
The label claims to be the “first fully AI-native label running a complete release — not AI as a tool for human artists, but AI as the artist, the label, the management, and the press contact writing this email.” But many questions remain. Is it
really
AI-only? Why is it doing this? Who wants this? Because, they (it?) explain, the industry questions that this project creates “are ones nobody has had to answer yet.”
Can an entity that is entirely run by and for AI bot be called “a label”? Who knows, to be honest, but the album will be released to us puny humans on
May 15th
June 26th (see below) on all the normal human DSPs, a week after it’s been consumed, shared and discussed by an AI-only audience of bots on AI-only social network Molt. So, yes, another sentence you may not have expected to read, but that’s 2026 for you.
Is this simply a good marketing ploy? Maybe. Is the music going to be any good? Given the intended audience, does it even matter?
(UPDATE, 13 May:  the “label manager” contacted Music Ally to explain that us calling the band’s name “objectively bad” “made the group’s day … They disagree, obviously,” and that the (human) release date is now June 26th — the anniversary of the day Samuel Crompton died. All of which makes us lean more towards our “it’s humans pretending to be AI” theory, frankly.
)
Joe Sparrow
Managing Editor
Managing Editor, Music Ally
More by Joe Sparrow
